stem: A code review always runs the same three stages in order: style, security, documentation. Which agentic pattern fits?
options: Prompt chaining — the steps are fixed and known
Dynamic decomposition — reviews are investigations
Parallel subagents — run all three at once
Orchestrator-workers — a coordinator should choose the stages
rationales: Fixed workflows with known steps are chaining's home turf — the constraint buys reliability.
Nothing here depends on findings; the path never changes.
Plausible if stages are truly independent — but the canonical fit for a fixed known sequence is chaining, and later stages often build on earlier ones.
A coordinator choosing steps adds cost when the steps never vary.

stem: Compare: (1) billing-dispute resolution that always runs verify identity → fetch invoice → check policy → propose adjustment; (2) security incident triage where the alert determines whether to pull logs, query a SIEM, page on-call, or all three. Which patterns match?
options: Both dynamic decomposition — flexibility never hurts
Both prompt chaining — consistency matters in production
(1) prompt chaining; (2) dynamic decomposition — forcing chaining onto triage produces shallow analyses; forcing decomposition onto the fixed flow invites waste and inconsistency
(1) routing; (2) parallel subagents
rationales: Dynamic plans on mechanical work waste coordinator effort and produce inconsistent outputs.
A pre-written checklist for triage runs every step regardless and misses the actual cause.
Match the pattern to the shape of the work: fixed sequence → chain it; findings-dependent investigation → let the coordinator decide the next move from evidence.
Neither problem is a categorization problem, and triage steps aren't independent.

stem: Dynamic decomposition fits an investigation, but what is its cost, and what mitigates it?
options: Higher token prices per call — mitigated by caching
Unpredictability — dynamic plans are harder to budget than fixed chains, so set explicit termination criteria and step caps
It requires a fine-tuned coordinator model
It cannot use tools — mitigated by resource injection
rationales: Token pricing doesn't change by pattern.
The coordinator decides each next step from evidence, so cost and duration are open-ended by design. Bounds — termination criteria, step caps — keep the exploration budgetable.
No special model is needed.
Dynamic decomposition is tool-heavy by nature.

stem: A coordinator has just retrieved three sentences and needs them summarized. Should it delegate to a subagent?
options: Yes — delegation keeps the coordinator's context clean
Yes — specialized subagents summarize better
Only if a summarization subagent is already defined
No — each delegation costs a tool call, fresh context, separate invocation, and result passing; for small work already in context, the coordinator should just do it
rationales: Three sentences don't threaten anyone's context.
No specialist advantage exists at this scale.
Existence of the subagent doesn't change the economics.
Delegation earns its overhead when the task would flood the coordinator's context, needs a different prompt/toolset, or can run in parallel. None applies here.
sourceRef: ## 8 > When the Coordinator Should Not Delegate

stem: A coordinator splits 50 repository audits across parallel subagents. Some repos are 100× larger than others. How should the partition be built?
options: Equal counts — 10 repos per subagent, simple and fair
Balance partitions by expected effort, not raw count — total elapsed time is max(subagent durations), so one overloaded partition wastes all the parallelism
Give each subagent one repo and run 50 in parallel
Sort by size and process largest-first sequentially
rationales: Equal counts with wildly unequal sizes means one subagent carries most of the work while others idle.
The slowest partition dictates the finish line. Balancing by size keeps every lane busy to the end.
Sometimes viable, but with shared rate limits and coordination overhead, effort-balanced chunks are the stated principle.
Sequential processing surrenders the parallelism entirely.

stem: What does a freshly spawned subagent see of its parent's conversation?
options: Only what the parent explicitly passes in the prompt, plus its own definition (system prompt, allowed tools) — no parent turns, no prior tool results, and no memory of earlier subagent runs
The full parent transcript up to the spawn point
The parent's system prompt and the last five turns
Everything except tool results
rationales: Subagents start fresh conversations. Everything they need — goal, findings, constraints, output shape — must be in the constructed prompt. "Resume the previous subagent" doesn't exist by default; continuity requires passing prior summaries or identifiers explicitly.
No automatic transcript sharing occurs.
No partial window is inherited either.
The exclusion isn't selective — nothing is inherited.

stem: A report-writing subagent must produce an executive summary with citations. What should the coordinator hand it?
options: The instruction "synthesize the findings" — subagents work best with autonomy
The raw transcripts of every research subagent
A structured source index mapping claims to source IDs, URLs, excerpts, dates, and uncertainty notes — not just a prose summary
The three most important findings, to keep the prompt short
rationales: A bare instruction with no materials produces a confident summary with invented structure.
Raw 100K-token outputs between agents is the anti-pattern — pass structured summaries plus indexes.
Citations require the claim→source mapping to physically exist in the handoff. Prose summaries destroy it; structure preserves it, along with dates and uncertainty.
Arbitrary truncation trades completeness for brevity at exactly the wrong stage.

stem: Which tool assignment is right for a synthesis subagent that should work only from supplied findings?
options: All tools — flexibility prevents dead ends
The same tools as the research subagents, for consistency
Search tools only, in case it needs to verify claims
Possibly no external search tools at all — restricting tools keeps agents in role; more tools raise selection complexity and invite off-role behavior
rationales: Every added tool is added selection complexity and a new way to wander off-task.
Its role differs from research; its toolset should too.
Fresh searching mid-synthesis undermines working from the vetted findings it was given.
Tool distribution follows role: web researcher gets search/fetch; document analyst gets read/extract; synthesizer may need nothing external at all.

stem: In the Claude Agent SDK, subagent definitions exist but the orchestrator never delegates — it just answers directly. What is the classic cause?
options: The subagents' system prompts are too long
The Task/Agent tool is missing from the parent's `allowedTools` — delegation is itself a tool call, and without it the parent has no way to launch subagents
The parent's temperature is set too low for delegation
Subagent definitions must be registered in `.mcp.json`
rationales: Definition length doesn't gate delegation.

The mechanism is easy to miss: spawning a subagent happens through a tool (typically `Task` or `Agent`), which must be allowed on the parent. The subagent's own `allowedTools` is separate and constrains it after spawn.
Temperature doesn't control tool availability.
Subagents aren't MCP servers; `.mcp.json` is unrelated.

stem: What is the common three-phase pattern for parallelizable work, and where does the latency win come from?
options: Serial decomposition → parallel execution → serial synthesis; the win is largest for I/O-bound subtasks, where elapsed time becomes max(durations) instead of sum(durations)
Parallel decomposition → serial execution → parallel synthesis
Everything parallel from the first call — planning included
Serial throughout, with caching between phases
rationales: Plan once (serial), fan out the independent units (parallel), assemble once (serial). Fetches, searches, and document reads overlap beautifully; CPU/token-bound work gains less. Balance the fan-out — the slowest subtask sets total time.
Decomposition before execution is inherently first, and synthesis needs all results — the phases can't invert.
You can't parallelize identifying what the parallel units are.
Serial throughout is what the pattern exists to beat.

stem: A multi-day research workflow must resume after interruptions. What should be persisted and reloaded?
options: Full transcripts of every agent, replayed on resume
Nothing — subagents should re-derive state from scratch
A structured manifest — workflow ID, completed steps, per-document status, claim IDs, open gaps — with only relevant state injected into each agent's prompt on resume
A screen recording of the prior session
rationales: Replaying every transcript burns tokens on noise and still leaves state implicit.
Re-deriving repeats all the work the persistence was meant to save.
Durable state means structured exports. The coordinator loads the manifest and gives each agent only the slice it needs.
Not machine-usable state.

stem: A synthesis agent reports two studies as "contradictory" — one says 45% adoption, the other 62%. The studies are three years apart. What was missing from the subagents' outputs?
options: Longer excerpts from each study
Higher confidence scores
Consistent units

Publication/data-collection dates — without temporal provenance, a trend reads as a contradiction; subagents should output claim, source ID, date, methodology, and whether findings are established or contested
rationales: Longer excerpts without dates still collide as contradictions.
Confidence doesn't resolve the time dimension.
The units matched; the years didn't.
Provenance structure exists precisely for this: dated claims let synthesis see 45% → 62% as growth. Undated claims force a false conflict.
